Quotation:
Several inflection points observed in our adoption curves temporally align with major ecosystem-level events in the Python community. For example, Python 2 officially reached its end-of-life on January 1, 2020, after which it no longer received security patches or official support, effectively removing Python 2 as a viable long-term platform and 

Walter Mendonça et al.: Preprint submitted to Elsevier Page 12 of 19

Leveraging social media news 

increasing pressure for migration to Python 3.3 This transition was reinforced by key ecosystem decisions, including the release of Django 2.0 in December 2017, which dropped support for Python 2 entirely and required Python 3, as well as the coordinated phase-out of Python 2.7 support by major scientific libraries such as NumPy, aligned with the Python core team’s deprecation timeline.45 In parallel, the maturation of the Python tooling ecosystem between 2016 and 2019 — particularly the emergence and consolidation of static type checking tools such as mypy, which builds on function annotations — lowered the practical cost of adopting modern language features.6 While these events do not establish causality, their temporal proximity to the observed acceleration in feature adoption suggests that external, ecosystem-driven pressures played an important contextual role in reducing long-standing inertia and enabling broader source code rejuvenation.
Documents: 34-SCP - “It Makes the Code Clearer”: Why Developers Adopt Modern Python Features in Open Source Projects
Codes: Framework/libraries dependencies motivate SCR efforts | language version support discontinuity
Concepts: Dependency and Framework Pressure | Language Evolution Enforcement

Quotation: A reason for changing is discontinuing, sometimes there’s no other way, even if the application is stable. For example, with Python, the language stopped receiving maintenance, it ends up not being purely an issue of being able to add new features, but also an issue of not receiving any patch if they find a security flaw in this interpreter version, and you end up having no choice but to update.

Firstly, I'd say necessity; if that new feature allows you to give a new aspect for the product or perhaps a performance or security improvement. Being in a corporative place puts some pressure in if you're using something that has surpassed its End-of-Life; it is not supported anymore, security breaches can appear and not be fixed. Many times we think something like: "Alright, I need to change this language version, I need to change this functionality because this version we use is not supported anymore.". Constantly, when you change versions, that feature evolves to other things, and you have to follow it.
Codes: Improve code performance | Improve code security | language version support discontinuity

Quotation: One of the most important aspects of evolving code, as the language evolves, is the need for migrating. One notorious language for giving developers headaches because of its ways of evolving is Scala. Its philosophy is: whatever becomes deprecated today, tomorrow will be lost. Code becomes unusable from one version to another, and the cost associated with leaving your code behind by two or three versions of the language is very high, both in time and energy spent in updating it. As a developer, it is very important to your day-to-day work to follow along with the language evolution, because it diminishes the cost of migrating an old codebase, which is hard to deal with, and saves you time down the road. Furthermore, it improves performance, since languages evolve in many aspects. Think about Ruby. From version 2.0 to version 3.0, it took an important step in improving performance. I went to a lecture by Koichi Sasada, who rewrote the entire garbage collector. He managed to make the garbage collector work three times faster, even though he said the goal was to make it ten times faster. Anyway, increasing its performance was an important improvement regarding its competitors, other faster and interpreted and compiled languages, like Python, Java, and C#. It is critically important that you are evolving your codebase as languages evolve so that you get new features, performance gains, security bug fixes, and avoid the trauma of migration.
Codes: Avoiding system obsolescence | Improve code performance | Improve code security | Lack of SCR may introduce technical debt
Concepts: Degradation effects | Performance and Efficiency | Preventing System Obsolescence | Reliability and Correctness
SubCategories: Challenges & Impediments | Code Quality Improvements | Drivers for Source Code Rejuvenation

Quotation:
K: So, from what you said, I assume that you have already used, or at least thought about using some tools like that to refactor...

P: Even developing such tools.

K: Oh, great. That's really great. Apart from your student’s project, at the end of the interview, we'd also like to hear about this idea, this ambition of yours. But today, according to you, there isn't any alternative that is safe or good enough for automatically rewriting large codes or industrial codes.

P: Yeah, because, in most cases, this does not involve (all) the syntactical changes, that involve semantical decisions.

K: So, would you say that the reason for the challenges or difficulties with those tools, and therefore the things that can block the usage in great masses of code on the codes that are high risk...

P: Blocking the refactoring tools?

K: Yeah. What could stop people from using them? You know, things that would make people insecure, or make them just not utilize them, and choose to rewrite manually the code when they had to do it?

P: Many factors. First of all, as I mentioned, it's very hard that you just start to rewrite the code, because you want to rewrite the code, you typically want to fix a bug or you want to modify some feature. So, and that's of course, won't be done by an automatic tool, anyway you have to do certain actions manually, and you modernize the code as a side effect. The other blocking factor is that C++ is an extremely complex language analysis, it’s much more complex than some of the other languages, let's say in Java, or C#, or similar. And to detect all the factors of where you have to use certain code to avoid any side effects, unwanted side effects is almost impossible at the moment. So, the refactoring tool is not mature enough to do this, and I'm not sure that they ever will be. It's not impossible, theoretically, but there are so many small issues that can put danger to the idea, which is just too tricky, let's say it this way.
Codes: Programming language complexity might hinder SCR efforts | SCR efforts may introduce bugs | SCR is a non-trivial effort
Concepts: Effort, Cost & Practical Constraints | Language Evolution Complexity & Instability | Risk, Uncertainty & Fear Factors

Quotation: You’ll need to pressure the upstream people so you can change too, you see? In case you’re working like this. So like, there’s the compiler change, and depending on how you use the language, in C++, you can incur your code in .... And then a compiler change can cause an incorrect execution, as you were counting on some behavior that’s not defined, or it can cause a performance loss. I’ve seen it happen in the last company that I worked for; we were working with version 14 and we tried to change into 17. When they started making performance tests, there was an (impact) on the performance. Until I left them, the cause remained unidentified. Normally, this is what happens: when you change compilers, the performance gets better. You see? The compiler people are not dumb, they also follow this way of thinking: if you’re making a change, it has to upgrade the security, performance, or usage. And they are very careful with C++ performance. I don’t know what happened, why the performance got worse, but it was detected that it did. And my guess is that someone was using a behavior anti error software in the code and it got a false performance. And maybe in our specific case it wouldn’t have caused any issues, but with the compiler change, the compiler people corrected the problem and it caused an impact. But I don’t know, we didn’t get to investigate it and I left the company before we solved this problem. But there was also another situation. We were on compiler, if I’m not mistaken, 5 or 4 point something, and we were going to compiler 10 - we were going to skip 5 versions. It’s risky, and there was no test coverage, increasing the risk even more.
Codes: Upgrading language implementation (compiler) might compromise performance

Quotation: It's an important factor, but not the major drive. Usually, we are working with quite complex systems, and we don't refactor them unless we see a really important advantage from this point of view. Let's see a few examples: in C++, well-known issues if somebody is not carefully programming and accidentally happens that you can make memory leaks or different kinds of memory-related issues, that C++ is very well known about. And at Microsoft review, I can send you the reference, it claims that in the last 15 years, more than 80% of the security patches were related to memory issues. Okay, that's at last 15 years on average. Now, of course, when C++ 11 introduced smart pointers, and other techniques, which we use for the danger of these problems, we happily, and almost immediately started to rewrite the code using these new features. But this is almost exceptional, it's typical if you have a large industry code, which works more or less fine. If you have no if you don’t have something exception or trigger, you don't touch that part. First of all, much more danger comes from rewriting the code and introducing new techniques, which you don't want to take if you have no... if there’s nothing... okay, not nothing, but it's not an exceptional advantage you hope for. If you experience regular memory issues, okay? Which demands lots of time and effort to be fixed, of course. You’re happy, accept these new language features and change them. If you see new language features that solve something more elegantly, you say: “Ok, next time, I will use that, but I won’t touch my code. It's working, I have no time and resources to rewrite it. And I don't want to take the risk of a new box.”.
Codes: Analyse costs/benefits before rejuvenate | Incremental SCR effort | Keep the systems running and evolutive maintenances are more important than SCR efforts | SCR requires careful planning

Quotation:
W: I see. And what reasons lead you to evolve code into supporting new language features?

R: Data protection, security.

W: I see. Security is the major reason that leads you to evolving code?

R: That’s right, memory leak.

W: Got it. Could you tell me more about it?

R: There used to be many constructions made based on vectors that, at some point, would corrupt the memory. And from that point on, it was a time bomb, that will explode sometime. But then you’d start to upload absurd data, and start getting complaints from above, people complaining that things that shouldn’t happen are happening from a memory leak. Other than that, there’s the concurrency issue, that gets access to everything and you have no guarantee that the data was being protected. Another problem with parallelism was having to postpone .... One piece of data waiting for another, then for another one, causing issues on the run and the application to freeze.
Codes: Help on bug prevention | Improve code security

Quotation:
P: It is possible. However, I don't think it is something vital, of great importance. It is nice to keep an eye out for it. But in case you already have a language, an automation set, programming, everything established and working, there is no immediate need for new things, in my opinion.

P: Except for security. Security is a problem. But when it comes to functionality, I don't think it is.

Well, I believe it’s more of a reactive thing. Reactive in the sense that it will be discontinued, or there’s a critical security bug, or the community no longer uses it. So, it’s not a very active thing, as in the past ten, fifteen years, in my opinion, the language has started evolving very quickly.

I work with junior developers who are amazing, by the way. Interviewer2 is part of this team who has impressed me. I have many friends that are a talent from a young age. Experience gives us another way of looking at things though. You, senior developer, think you are doing the best, but the tool presents a better way of doing it. There are amazing tools for this, the linters help a lot in this aspect. For example, Ruby has a set of Gems that help you detect problematic code and security holes, and all that helps you evolve your code. So, use it. Not all languages have tools as good as these that are usually the most visible in the community. An example of a language that is perhaps one of the most used: Apple's Swift. But I can't tell if this is a good thing. Firstly, because I think that everything involving Apple is closed in a bubble in which I am not inserted, but it is a language that today is among the top ten most used languages. I can't tell you if it has such good tools. But there are languages like Ruby, and Java which have the Sonar project, from Sonar Type, if I am not mistaken. Microsoft invests heavily in its tools, JetBrains does too; Google has an excellent code style that you can include in your tool; it reviews your code. Use it because it's worth it. Sometimes it can get in your way, but you can limit it, like: "I'm going to ignore this rule because I don't need it.". Ruby's RoboCop is like that, you have to customize it, or it will point out very trivial aspects, but overall it's wonderful.
Codes: Reliance on static analysis to detect SCR opportunities
Concepts: Selective and Human-Controlled Use of Tool Support
SubCategories: Tooling Support for Source Code Rejuvenation
